title: The wrapper that was missing
checkLabels.g1: The code must be valid HTML
checkLabels.t1: There must be an html wrapper
checkLabels.t4: The content must still be there
checkLabels.t5: The heading must be inside body
checkLabels.t6: The sentence must be inside body
why.not-code: There is not a single closed tag. Either you wrote something that is not HTML, or a tag is open and never closed.
why.no-html: There is no wrapper. Everything on the page lives inside one tag — it wraps both the head and the body.
why.no-head: There is no head. It is empty for now, and that is exactly why it is easy to skip. But in two lessons you will need it — the tab name and the encoding go there.
why.no-body: There is no body. Everything a person sees lives inside it. Your heading and your sentence belong there.
why.lost-content: The heading is gone. The task is to WRAP what you already have — not to delete it and start over.
why.content-in-head: The wrapper is there, but something sits in the wrong half.
hint2.not-code: Go top to bottom, tag by tag. Every opening tag is looking for its closing tag.
hint3.not-code: Remember: head ends before body begins. They are siblings, not one inside the other. It is <head></head><body></body>.
hint2.no-html: All your code goes inside <html> ... </html>. Everything else lives inside it.
hint2.no-head: <head> is the first thing inside <html>. It is empty for now — just <head></head>.
hint2.no-body: <body> comes AFTER </head>, not inside it. They are siblings.
hint2.lost-content: Do not delete. Wrap. The heading and the sentence you already have must stay — just inside <body>.
hint2.content-in-head: Look at what sits between <head> and </head>. That part is the invisible one — it holds information ABOUT the page, not the page itself.
hint3.content-in-head: The skeleton in the lesson has an empty <head> and an empty <body>. Look at it again. Your heading and your sentence show up on screen — so where do they belong?
hint4.content-in-head: Cut the two lines with h1 and p out of head. Put them between <body> and </body>.
blocks.0.text: So far you have written bare text with tags, and it worked. The browser is forgiving — it swallows whatever you give it and tries to show it.
blocks.1.text: But a real page has a wrapper. And it is not a formality.
blocks.4.text: <html> wraps everything — this is the document itself. Inside it there are exactly two things, and they are completely different in meaning.
blocks.6.text: <body> is the page a person sees. Everything you want to appear on screen goes here. Headings, sentences, images — everything.
blocks.7.text: <head> is the opposite: nothing in it is visible. It holds information ABOUT the page — what it is called, what language it is in, who wrote it. Like a label on a box: not part of the contents, but it tells you what is inside.
blocks.9.text: Take your page and wrap it properly: <html> on the outside, <head> empty for now, and everything visible inside <body>.
blocks.10.text: Run. Nothing will change on screen. That is exactly why it was easy to skip — and exactly why you must get used to writing it.
